feed.title: Combined summary - One Time Signatures as an Advantage?

entry.title: Combined summary - One Time Signatures as an Advantage?
entry.summary: The discussion in the recent exchanges among members of the Bitcoin Development Mailing List has centered on various signature schemes and their potential standardization by NIST. A stateless, post-quantum-secure hash-based signature scheme known as SLH-DSA is being considered for standardization. This scheme, although capable of signing multiple messages, incurs a significant increase in signature size, with signatures measuring up to 7,888 bytes under specific parameters (n=16, w=16). Alternatives like XMSS reduce this to 900 bytes when statelessness is not a requirement, and further reductions are achievable through one-time signature schemes such as WOTS+, which can bring the size down to just 560 bytes. There has been some interest in whether these recommendations, particularly stateful hash-based schemes recommended by NIST, will lead to full standardization and FIPS compliance. The conversation touches on whether Bitcoin might adopt technologies like SHRINCS before they receive formal NIST certification. SHRINCS, described in more detail [here](https://delvingbitcoin.org/t/shrincs-324-byte-stateful-post-quantum-signatures-with-static-backups/2158), offers compact signatures with the flexibility of signing multiple times if necessary, which seems advantageous especially in scenarios where state is lost or a stateful key is excessively used. However, concerns were raised about the suitability of one-time signature schemes for Bitcoin. These schemes are not ideal for multi-user transactions as they could disrupt the process and require a second signature, potentially leading to lost funds or keys if addresses are reused. Furthermore, one-time signature schemes do not support transaction replacement, which is favored by zero-confirmation enthusiasts. In summary, while there are promising developments in signature technology that could enhance security and efficiency in blockchain applications, careful consideration is needed regarding their implementation in Bitcoin. The trade-offs between signature size, state requirements, and usability in diverse transaction scenarios must be carefully balanced to avoid compromising functionality or user experience.
